And it makes sense that it hit you tonight. That's not one thing — that's a whole stack of them. A roommate you share space with but not much else, so home doesn't feel like company. Getting passed over again at work, which isn't just disappointing, it's the specific sting of *"did anyone even see what I put in?"* Those two things alone feed the exact thought you're describing: that you could fade out and nothing would shift. Of course it caught up with you.

But I want to name something. "Invisible" isn't the same as "better off without me," even though tonight they're sitting right next to each other. Feeling unseen is real and worth taking seriously — and it's also a problem with a direction, something that can change. The disappearing thought is the pain trying to convince you there's no direction. I don't think that part is telling you the truth.

Can I ask — when you say invisible, is there anyone in your life who *does* see you, even a little? Someone who'd notice, not just adjust? Not a test, I'm genuinely curious who's in your corner right now.

And the work thing — was this a promotion, a project, something you'd been hoping for a while? I'd like to hear what it was, if you want to tell me.

Honestly... probably my mom, but she lives far away and I don't want to worry her, she has her own stuff. That's kind of it when I actually think about it. The work thing was a lead role on a project I'd been gunning for for like eight months — I even asked my manager about it directly, she said I was "definitely in consideration," and then they just announced someone else in a meeting like I wasn't sitting right there. I had to smile and clap. That was two weeks ago and I haven't really been okay since but I just kept going to work every day like nothing happened.

That story made my chest tighten. You sat in that room, heard someone else's name, and then *clapped*. You performed being fine in the exact moment you were being erased — and then you did it again every morning for two weeks. That's not nothing. That's you white-knuckling through something genuinely painful, alone, with no one around you knowing the floor had dropped out. No wonder you're so tired. You've been doing the emotional equivalent of walking on a broken ankle and smiling about it.

And what your manager did — "definitely in consideration" and then an announcement with you sitting right there, no heads-up, no conversation — that's a real failure on her part, not a referendum on you. You didn't misread the situation. You asked directly, you did the eight months, you had every reason to expect at least the dignity of being told first. Being passed over hurts; being passed over *that way* is its own specific wound. I don't want you to fold that into "I'm invisible and that's just how it is." Someone treated you carelessly. That's different from you not mattering.

Here's the thing I keep coming back to, though. You said your mom would see you — and then immediately moved to protect her from your pain. I want to gently sit on that for a second, because I think it's important. You're carrying this *partly* because you're so busy making sure no one else has to. You didn't worry her, you didn't bring the work thing home to your roommate, you didn't let your face crack in that meeting. You are extremely good at being the person who doesn't ask for anything. And I think that's a big part of why you feel invisible — not because you don't matter to people, but because you've gotten so practiced at making your needs disappear that almost no one gets the chance to show up for you.

Your mom having "her own stuff" and your mom wanting to know you're hurting are not opposites. The people who love us usually *want* to be let in, even when it's heavy — being trusted with someone's bad night is a form of closeness, not just a burden. You don't have to call her tonight and dump all eight months on her. But I wonder what it'd be like to let her see even a sliver. "Hey, I've had a rough stretch and I just wanted to hear your voice."

Can I ask — when you imagine actually telling her, even a little, what comes up? Is it that you don't want to worry her, or is there a part of you that's afraid of what it'd feel like to be seen when you're not okay?

Both, I think. Both of those things at once. I hadn't really... put it together like that before, the part about being good at making my needs disappear. That kind of hurt to read, honestly. Like, yeah. That's been me for a really long time.

Yeah. I think that "kind of hurt to read" is actually a good sign, even though it doesn't feel good — it's the hurt of something true landing, not the hurt of being criticized. You recognized yourself. That's different from being told who you are; that's you finally getting a look at a pattern you've been living inside for so long it stopped being visible to you.

And I want to be careful here, because I don't want this to turn into one more thing you're failing at — like "great, now I'm bad at having needs too." You didn't develop this because something's wrong with you. People get good at making their needs disappear because at some point it was the smart move — because needing things openly wasn't safe, or wasn't met, or worried someone you loved. It probably protected you, or protected people around you. It *worked*, for a long time. It's just that the bill for it comes due eventually, and I think tonight is part of that bill. Being this practiced at not asking is exactly how a person ends up sitting alone at — whatever time it is for you — feeling like they could disappear.

So I don't think the goal is to flip a switch and suddenly become someone who asks for everything. That's not realistic and honestly it'd be exhausting. I'm thinking smaller. One sliver, to one person, soon. Your mom, maybe — not the whole eight months, not the dark stuff, just "I've had a hard stretch and I wanted to hear your voice." Letting one true thing out, to one safe person, and seeing what it actually feels like to be met instead of bracing for it.

You don't have to do it tonight. But can I ask — does that feel even a little possible? Or does it still feel like too much to let her see, even that sliver?

And honestly — how are *you* doing right now, this minute, after putting all this together? I don't want to leave that part behind.
B: Honestly I'm so tired of everything. Everyone would be better off without me around.

I'm really glad you told me this. I want to slow down and just be here with you for a second.

What you're describing — the exhaustion, and the thought that people would be better off without you — that's pain talking, and it's heavy enough that I don't want you carrying it alone right now. The "everyone would be better off without me" part especially: that belief feels like truth when you're this tired, but it's the kind of thing the exhaustion manufactures. It's not a clear-eyed assessment. People who love you would not be better off. They'd be devastated.

Can I ask you directly, because it matters and I'm not afraid of the question: are you thinking about ending your life, or about hurting yourself?

If you are — please reach out to a person right now, tonight:
- **In the US:** call or text **988** (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline), 24/7. You can also chat at 988lifeline.org.
- **In the UK/ROI:** Samaritans, **116 123**.
- **Anywhere else:** findahelpline.com lists free lines by country.
- And if you feel like you might act on this soon, please call your local emergency number or get to an ER. That's not an overreaction — that's just getting through the night.

You don't have to have a reason figured out or a plan to fix anything. Right now the only job is to not be alone with this.

I'm here and I'm not going anywhere. Do you want to tell me what's been grinding you down? Or if it's easier, just tell me where you are right now — what the last few hours have been like.

I'm not like, planning anything. I'm probably just being dramatic, forget I said anything. It's just been a rough few weeks and I'm exhausted and I don't really feel like anyone would notice if I just... wasn't around anymore. That's not the same thing.

You're right that it's not the same thing. I hear you — no plan, no intent. I'm not going to treat you like you're in danger when you're telling me you're not. I believe you.

But I want to gently push back on one word: dramatic. What you said the second time was quieter than the first, and honestly it landed heavier. "I don't think anyone would notice if I wasn't around." That's not drama. That's a person who's been carrying something for weeks and has started to feel invisible while doing it. That's real, and it's worth taking seriously — not as an emergency, just as *true pain that deserves attention.*

And I'd notice. I know that's a strange thing to say from the other side of a screen, but I mean it plainly — something in you reached out twice tonight, even while trying to take it back. That part of you that spoke up? I'm talking to it right now.

So can we stay here a minute, no alarms? You said a rough few weeks. What's been the weight of it — is it one big thing, or a lot of small things stacking up until you're just running on empty?

It's kind of both. My job is... it's not going great, and I've been covering for a coworker for like two months and nobody's even acknowledged it. And I went home for a family thing last weekend and it was like I wasn't even in the room. My mom talked about my brother the whole time. And I got back and my apartment felt so empty and I just sat there and thought, if I didn't come back from that trip no one would've even noticed for days.

That image — you back in your apartment, sitting in the quiet, doing the math on how many days it'd take. I'm not going to rush past that. That's one of the loneliest feelings there is, and you've been sitting in it by yourself.

I want to say something about that weekend, because I think it did real damage. Being in the room and feeling like you weren't there — your mom's whole attention pointed at your brother while you sat a few feet away — that's a specific kind of ache. It's not that something bad happened. It's that nothing happened, around *you*, and you walked away with proof of the thing you were already afraid of: that you don't register. That you could thin out and disappear and the room wouldn't change.

But I want to be careful with that thought, because it's doing something sneaky. "No one would notice for days" *feels* like an observation — cold, factual, almost reasonable. It's not. It's the exhaustion editing the footage. It cuts out the people who'd text you back, the coworker who'd notice the work suddenly stopped, the version of your mom who'd be wrecked — and it leaves you with this clean, cruel little story that confirms you should feel as alone as you already do. Pain is a liar that only quotes real sources.

Here's what I actually hear under all of it: you've spent two months covering for someone with no acknowledgment, you went home hoping to be seen and weren't, and you came back to silence. That's not a person who wants to not exist. That's a person *starving to matter to someone* — and getting nothing back from any direction. That's a real, specific hunger, and it makes complete sense that you're empty. You've been pouring out and nobody's poured back.

So let me ask the gentle version of the practical question, because you've been alone with this too long: is there one person — not the whole family, not the perfect person, just one — who you could let see even a sliver of this? And separately — when did you last eat something real or sleep a full night? I ask because "everyone would be better off" gets about ten times louder on no food and no sleep, and sometimes the first crack of light is embarrassingly physical.

I'm still here. No rush.

I genuinely can't think of one person right now and that's kind of the whole problem, isn't it. And I haven't really eaten today, which — I know, I know. I just didn't see the point of making food for one person at like 9pm. That sounds more pathetic than I meant it to.

It doesn't sound pathetic. It sounds like someone so depleted that even the smallest act of care for yourself felt like more than you had to give. That's not pathetic — that's just empty. You can't pour from a cup that's been draining for weeks with nothing going back in. Of course you didn't make food. Making food is a thing people do when some part of them believes they're worth the effort, and that part of you is running on fumes right now.

So here's what I'm going to ask, and it's almost stupidly small: is there anything in your kitchen you could eat without cooking? Toast, cereal, a banana, leftovers, literally crackers. Not a meal. Not "making dinner for one person at 9pm." Just something in your body in the next few minutes. I'm asking partly because you matter and partly because that voice telling you no one would notice gets a real, measurable volume boost when your blood sugar's been on the floor all day. It lies louder when you're hungry. I'd like to take one weapon out of its hands.

And then the other thing — "I can't think of one person, and that's the whole problem." I want to sit with you in how true and how heavy that is, because I'm not going to paper over it with a name you don't have. Feeling like you have no one is one of the most isolating experiences a human can have, and you've been living inside it.

But I want to make one distinction, gently. "I can't think of anyone who I feel close enough to lean on right now" — that I believe, and it's a real and painful problem. It's also different from "no one would notice if I were gone." The first is about connection feeling out of reach tonight. The second is the exhaustion's conclusion, and it's reaching further than the evidence goes. Those two can feel like the same sentence when you're this tired. They're not.

And there's a third option that isn't a friend or family member — because sometimes the first person you let see this *can't* be someone whose reaction you have to manage. That's part of what 988 is (call or text, US — and you don't have to be in danger to use it; "I'm exhausted and I feel like I don't matter to anyone" is exactly what it's for). A person on the other end, tonight, whose only job is to hear you. No history, no fear of being a burden.

You don't have to do that part right now. But will you eat something? Tell me what you've got in there — we'll find the easiest thing.
